   ECG WAVEFORM TELEMETRY PREVIEW

ECG WAVEFORM TELEMETRY PREVIEW 
 
   CLINICAL FINDINGS & MORPHOLOGICAL ANALYSIS 
• Rhythm: Regular sinus mechanism pacing at 104 beats per minute.

• P Waves: Normal configuration; 1:1 AV conduction confirmed across all leads. 
• ST Segment: Flatline baseline; no acute ST-segment elevation or depression identified to 
suggest active transmural 
• ischemia or acute coronary event. 
• T Waves: Inversion noted in lateral leads V5 and V6, which may imply chronic myocardial strain 
or left ventricular 
• hypertrophy substrate. 
   DIAGNOSTIC IMPRESSION 
 
• Sinus Tachycardia at 104 bpm.  
• Nonspecific T-wave inversions in the lateral leads.

• Sinus Tachycardia at 104 bpm.  
• Nonspecific T-wave inversions in the lateral leads.  
Note: Patient was reported as highly anxious during the clinical encounter, which likely accounts for the heart 
rate acceleration. No classic signs of acute ischemia present on current trace. 
 
 
Electronically signed off by attending cardiologist                                                 Dr. Vance Sterling, MD, FACC

PATHOLOGIST INTERPRETATION & COMMENTS
Remarks: Microcytic mild anemia noted. Elevated Total Leukocyte Count (WBC) combined with Neutrophilia strongly
indicates an acute bacterial infection or inflammatory response. Clinical correlation with presenting symptoms (e.g., fever,
localized pain) is recommended.

STUDY DETAILS
Examination: Chest PA and Lateral Views
Clinical Indication: Chronic productive cough, mild dyspnea, and low-grade pyrexia for 1 week.

Clinical Indication: Chronic productive cough, mild dyspnea, and low-grade pyrexia for 1 week.
DIAGNOSTIC IMAGES ATTACHED
[CHEST_PA_VIEW_IMAGE_ID_99214]
Embedded DICOM Reference Preview Placeholder
FINDINGS
Lungs: There is a distinct, patchy alveolar opacity / consolidation noted in the right lower lobe, consistent with localized
airspace disease. The left lung field appears clear with normal vascular markings. No evidence of cavitary lesions or diffuse
interstitial fibrosis.

interstitial fibrosis. 
Pleura: Right costophrenic angle shows mild blunting, highly suggestive of a small reactive pleural effusion. Left costophrenic
and cardiophrenic angles are sharp and well-defined. 
Mediastinum & Heart: The cardiomediastinal contour is within normal limits. Cardiac size is normal with no cardiomegaly.
Hilar structures are clear. Trachea is midline. 
Bones & Soft Tissues: Visualized thoracic skeleton shows no acute fractures or destructive osseous lesions. Soft tissues

are unremarkable. 
IMPRESSION
1. Right lower lobe consolidation: Highly indicative of acute lobar pneumonia. 
2. Mild right-sided pleural effusion: Likely parapneumonic in origin. 
Confidential - Medical Record
Page 1 of 2

Recommendation: Clinical correlation with inflammatory biomarkers and follow-up radiograph post-antibiotic
treatment in 4-6 weeks to ensure complete resolution.
